quotes.verbatim_quote:
Chord tones (1,3,5 ) are good notes to begin a phrase with. Know where they are on your
instrument.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Concrete rule for beginning improvised phrases.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Do you want to use pick-ups? One, or more? If so, make sure they lead to the first strong beat!
The strong beats are beats 1 and 3.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines strong beats and pick-up resolution rule.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
What rhythm are you going to play initially? Does your mind already "HEAR" the notes/pitches

in rhythm? Can you actually play them? Remember, your first phrase represents the first several
words or idea of a sentence. Think clearly before you begin.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Mental pre-hearing as prerequisite to execution.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
8th and 16th notes build tension

Rests, whole and half-notes create relaxation
The pretty notes for major and dominant 7th chords/scales are the 6,7,9, and #4.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines color tones for major/dominant 7th sounds.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The pretty notes for minor chords/scales are 4,6,7, and 9th. These notes create tension and should
be used in the over-all tension-release process.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Color tones for minor harmony tied to tension-release.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
1. Play Ist note, root/tonic note of each chord/scale
2. Play first 2 notes of each scale
3. Play first 3 notes of each scale
4. Play first five notes of each scale
5. Play triad (1, 3 and 5 of the scale)
6. Play 7th chords (1, 3, 5 and 7th tones of each scale)
7. Play 9th chords (1, 3, 5, 7 and 9th tones of each scale)
8. Play the entire scale up and down
9. Play 6th chords (1, 3, 5 and 6th tones of the scale)
10. Play up scale to the 9th and back down the chord tones
11. Play up 9th chord and then come back down the scale
12. Play scale in broken thirds up and down. (1,3,2,4,3,5,4,6,5,7,6,8,7,9,8 then backwards)
quotes.why_load_bearing: 12-step memorization sequence — the method's central technical exercise.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
If you were to use the above procedure for the 12-bar blues you would need 12 choruses to
complete all twelve exercises. By the twelfth chorus your mind will be HEARING the chord/scale
progression in advance. Your fingers will begin to go to the right notes automatically, almost

without having to tell them.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Telos of the memorization procedure — auditory anticipation.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Play scale in broken thirds up and down. (1,3,2,4,3,5,4,6,5,7,6,8,7,9,8 then backwards)
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specific intervallic pattern for broken-thirds drill.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Learn the rules, then you get to break them.
Be sure you learn them well or when you break them, others will think you never knew them.

Play "drop the needle" or "raise the laser" on the play-a-long recording.
Try to figure out which track is playing just by listening. Surprise yourself.
